content: I want to make a really good spaghetti carbonara tonight. What's the secret to getting the sauce silky instead of scrambled?

The trick is temperature control. Whisk the eggs and grated Pecorino together off the heat, then toss them with the hot pasta away from the burner, adding a splash of starchy pasta water to loosen it. The residual heat cooks the eggs into a creamy sauce without scrambling them.
Should I use guanciale or pancetta, and do I need cream?

It really depends on the dish you're making and the flavor profile you're aiming for. Guanciale and pancetta are both Italian cured meats, but they have some differences. Guanciale is made from pork jowl and has a more delicate, unsmoked flavor, while pancetta is made from pork belly and has a richer, more intense flavor.

If you're making a classic carbonara, guanciale is the traditional choice, and it pairs beautifully with eggs, parmesan, and black pepper. Pancetta can also work well, but it might give the dish a slightly different character.

As for cream, traditional carbonara doesn't actually contain any cream. The creaminess comes from the eggs, cheese, and pasta water. Adding cream can make the dish richer and more indulgent, but it's not necessary, and some people might argue that it alters the authentic flavor and texture of the carbonara.

What's the dish you're planning to make, and what's your personal preference when it comes to creamy sauces?

I've had it traditional style before, and while I love it, I'm curious to see how it would turn out with a slightly creamier sauce. I've heard some people add a small amount of heavy cream or half-and-half to give it a richer texture, and I'm tempted to try that.

But at the same time, I don't want to overpower the delicate flavor of the guanciale, which I've decided to use instead of pancetta. I've read that guanciale has a more subtle, nutty flavor that pairs really well with the eggs and parmesan, so I don't want to overwhelm that with too much cream.

Do you think it's possible to strike a balance between the two, or would you recommend sticking with the traditional recipe? And have you ever tried adding any other ingredients to carbonara to give it a unique twist, such as mushrooms or asparagus?

content:
I completely understand your dilemma. You want to enhance the creaminess of the sauce without overpowering the delicate flavor of the guanciale. I think it's definitely possible to strike a balance between the two. If you do decide to add a small amount of heavy cream or half-and-half, I would recommend starting with a very small amount, maybe just a tablespoon or two, and then tasting as you go. That way, you can adjust the creaminess to your liking without overpowering the other flavors.

As for other ingredients, I think mushrooms could be a great addition to carbonara, especially if you use a variety like porcini or cremini that has a rich, earthy flavor. The key is to cook them until they're nice and caramelized, so they add a depth of flavor to the dish without making it feel too heavy or overpowering. Asparagus could also work well, especially if you blanch it briefly to preserve its texture and flavor. Just be sure to chop it into small pieces so it distributes evenly throughout the pasta.

One other ingredient you might consider adding is some fresh parsley or basil. A sprinkle of chopped herbs can add a bright, freshness to the dish that helps balance out the richness of the eggs and guanciale. And if you want to get really creative, you could even try adding some other ingredients like roasted garlic, lemon zest, or grated truffle. The key is to experiment and find the combination that works best for you.

But I have to ask, what made you decide to use guanciale instead of pancetta? Have you worked with guanciale before, or is this your first time trying it out?

content:
I completely agree with you on the uniqueness of guanciale's flavor profile. The delicate, unsmoked quality is definitely a game-changer in carbonara. And I'm with you on pancetta being a bit too smoky at times - it's all about finding that balance.

Regarding the saltiness of guanciale, I totally get what you mean. High-quality, cured guanciale can be quite salty, and it's easy to overpower the other flavors in the dish. For me, it's all about balancing the saltiness with acidity and brightness. A squeeze of fresh lemon juice or a splash of white wine can help cut through the richness and saltiness of the guanciale. I also like to use a light hand when seasoning the dish, and instead of adding more salt, I'll add a bit more black pepper or red pepper flakes to add depth without adding more salt.

Another trick I use is to cook the guanciale until it's nice and crispy, then chop it up into smaller pieces. This helps distribute the flavor more evenly throughout the dish, and the crispy texture adds a nice contrast to the creamy eggs and pasta. And of course, tasting and adjusting as you go is key - it's always better to err on the side of caution and add more salt later, rather than over-salting the dish from the start.

I'm curious, have you ever tried using different types of pasta with your carbonara? I've found that some shapes, like spaghetti or bucatini, work really well with the creamy sauce, while others, like pappardelle or fettuccine, can be a bit too heavy. What's your go-to pasta shape for carbonara?

That being said, I do think that traditional guanciale or bacon is a big part of what makes carbonara so special, so I'm not sure I'd want to give it up entirely. content:
The cream debate is a contentious one, isn't it? I've had carbonara with cream before, and while it can add a richness and indulgence to the dish, I tend to agree with you that it can also make it feel heavy and over-powering. Traditional carbonara is all about the harmony of eggs, cheese, and guanciale, and the cream can disrupt that delicate balance. Plus, the starches in the pasta are already coated in a creamy egg mixture, so adding more cream can make it feel overly rich and cloying.

That being said, I do think there's a time and a place for creamy carbonara. If you're using a high-quality, farm-fresh cream that's not too heavy or overpowering, it can add a lovely depth and luxury to the dish. But it's all about restraint, I think. A small amount of cream can enhance the existing flavors, but too much can overwhelm them.

It's interesting that you mention the Italian approach to black pepper, by the way. I've found that Italians tend to be very generous with their black pepper, and it's not uncommon to see a carbonara dish with a visible dusting of pepper on top. It's almost like the pepper is being used as a seasoning and a textural element, adding a nice crunch and depth to the dish.

Speaking of texture, have you ever experimented with adding any other ingredients to carbonara to change up the texture? I've had carbonara with toasted breadcrumbs or pangrattato, which adds a lovely crunch and nuttiness to the dish.
